description: Fix bugs, resource leaks, and safety issues in the MCP server core: partial state update in reloadCore, removeLocalPlugin false-success on write failure, missing HTTP body size limit, stale failure entries in file watcher, fire-and-forget extension.reload using request instead of notification, and 30s domain resolution blocking browser tool calls.

userStories.title: Fix reloadCore partial state update on loadSecret failure
userStories.description: As a platform operator, I want reloadCore to not leave state partially updated when loadSecret fails, so that the server's in-memory state is always consistent.
acceptanceCriteria: If loadSecret() throws, none of the state fields (registry, toolConfig, browserToolPolicy, pluginPaths, discoveryErrors, permissions, skipConfirmation) are updated — all remain at their pre-reload values
The error log message accurately describes what happened (not 'keeping previous state' when state was partially updated)
If loadSecret() succeeds but returns a different secret, secretChanged is correctly computed and triggers extension reload
userStories.notes:
File: platform/mcp-server/src/reload.ts, lines 186-218.

The bug: state mutations happen at lines 191-197 (state.registry = registry, state.toolConfig = {...}, etc.), then loadSecret() is called at line 215. If loadSecret() throws, the catch at line 217 runs, but registry/toolConfig/etc. are ALREADY updated. The log says 'keeping previous state' which is incorrect.

Fix approach: Collect all new values into local variables first (config, registry, secret), then assign them all to state at once AFTER all async operations succeed. Move `state.wsSecret = await loadSecret()` into the same try block but BEFORE the state mutations, or give loadSecret its own independent try-catch so a secret failure doesn't prevent config/registry updates (which is the current intent). The cleanest fix: wrap loadSecret in its own try-catch that logs the error and keeps the old secret, while letting config/registry updates proceed. This makes the catch block's 'keeping previous state' message accurate for config/discovery failures, and loadSecret failures are handled independently.

userStories.title: Fix removeLocalPlugin reporting success when atomicWrite fails
userStories.description: As a user removing a local plugin, I want the removal to report failure when the config file write fails, so that I know the plugin was not actually removed.
acceptanceCriteria: If atomicWrite throws inside the mutex closure, removeLocalPlugin returns false (not true)
The configWriteMutex is properly reset so subsequent writes are not blocked
If atomicWrite succeeds, removeLocalPlugin still returns true
userStories.notes:
File: platform/mcp-server/src/plugin-management.ts, lines 361-441.

The bug: `removed` is set to `true` at lines 409/419 BEFORE `atomicWrite` at line 431. If atomicWrite throws, the .catch() at line 433 reassigns `state.configWriteMutex = Promise.resolve()` and re-throws. But the outer `await state.configWriteMutex` at line 439 now awaits the REASSIGNED Promise.resolve() (not the original rejected promise), so it resolves successfully and returns `removed = true` even though the write failed.

Fix approach: Restructure so the async closure's return value (or rejection) is properly captured. One approach: store the closure's promise in a separate variable and await that directly, rather than re-awaiting the mutex. Another approach: use a result variable that is set to true only AFTER atomicWrite succeeds. The simplest fix: move `removed = true` to after the `atomicWrite` call (after line 431), so it's only set when the write actually succeeds.

userStories.title: Add HTTP request body size limit to collectBody
userStories.description: As a security-conscious operator, I want the MCP server to reject oversized HTTP request bodies, so that a local attacker cannot exhaust server memory.
acceptanceCriteria: collectBody enforces a maximum body size of 10MB (matching the WebSocket maxPayload)
If the body exceeds the limit, the request stream is destroyed and the promise rejects with a descriptive error
The HTTP request handler catches this rejection and returns a 413 (Payload Too Large) response
Normal-sized requests continue to work correctly
userStories.notes:
File: platform/mcp-server/src/server-node.ts, lines 99-105.

The current collectBody function reads all chunks with no size check. The WebSocket server already has maxPayload: 10 * 1024 * 1024 at line 227, so use the same 10MB limit for HTTP bodies.

Fix approach: Track accumulated size in collectBody. When it exceeds 10MB, call req.destroy() and reject with an error. In handleHttpRequest (lines 111-137), catch this specific error and return a 413 response.

Also define a constant MAX_BODY_SIZE = 10 * 1024 * 1024 near the top of the file, matching the WebSocket constant.

userStories.title: Filter stale failure entries in handlePendingPluginChange
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want the /health endpoint to not show my plugin as both loaded and failed after it recovers from a build error.
acceptanceCriteria: When handlePendingPluginChange successfully loads a plugin, the new registry does not contain a failure entry for that plugin's path
The /health endpoint shows the plugin only in pluginDetails (loaded), not in failedPlugins
Other failure entries for different plugins are preserved
userStories.notes:
File: platform/mcp-server/src/file-watcher.ts, lines 371-401.

At line 389: `state.registry = buildRegistry(updatedPlugins, [...state.registry.failures])` — the failures list still includes the entry for `pluginDir` that just loaded successfully.

Fix: Filter failures before passing to buildRegistry:
```
const updatedFailures = state.registry.failures.filter(f => f.path !== pluginDir);
state.registry = buildRegistry(updatedPlugins, updatedFailures);
```

userStories.title: Send extension.reload as a notification instead of a request
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the extension.reload message to be a proper JSON-RPC notification (no id field), so that the protocol semantics match the fire-and-forget intent.
acceptanceCriteria: sendExtensionReload sends a JSON-RPC notification (no id field) instead of a request
The extension.reload message has jsonrpc: '2.0' and method: 'extension.reload' but no id
The return type and behavior remain the same (returns boolean indicating send success)
userStories.notes:
File: platform/mcp-server/src/extension-protocol.ts, lines 502-509.

Current code creates an id via getNextRequestId() and sends a message with method + id (which is a JSON-RPC request expecting a response). But no pending dispatch is registered, so the response is silently dropped. The intent is fire-and-forget.

Fix: Remove the id field. Change the message type from JsonRpcRequest to JsonRpcNotification. Remove the getNextRequestId() call. The sendToExtension call stays the same.

Also check platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/reload-extension.ts (lines 20-33) which has the same pattern — sends a request with id but never registers a pending dispatch. Apply the same fix there. Note: the reload-extension.ts test at platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/reload-extension.test.ts may need updating if it checks for an id in the sent message.

userStories.title: Use short timeout for domain resolution in resolveToolDomain
userStories.description: As an AI agent using browser tools, I want domain resolution to fail fast when a tab is unresponsive, so that browser tool calls don't silently block for 30 seconds.
acceptanceCriteria: resolveToolDomain uses a timeout of 5 seconds (not the default 30s DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS) for the browser.getTabInfo dispatch
If the tab is unresponsive, resolveToolDomain returns null within ~5 seconds instead of ~30 seconds
The catch block still returns null on timeout (same behavior, just faster)
userStories.notes:
File: platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-tool-dispatch.ts, lines 70-82.

The current code calls `dispatchToExtension(state, 'browser.getTabInfo', { tabId: tabIdArg })` with no options, so it uses the default 30s timeout from DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS.

The dispatchToExtension function at platform/mcp-server/src/extension-protocol.ts:119-173 creates the timeout at line 145 using DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS. To use a shorter timeout, there are two approaches:

1. Add a `timeoutMs` option to DispatchOptions and use it in dispatchToExtension instead of the default DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS. Then pass { label: 'browser.getTabInfo', timeoutMs: 5000 } in resolveToolDomain.

2. Or, wrap the dispatchToExtension call in a Promise.race with a shorter timeout and catch the race.

Approach 1 is cleaner and more reusable. Add an optional `timeoutMs?: number` field to the DispatchOptions interface, and use `opts.timeoutMs ?? DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS` for the setTimeout duration at line 145. Then pass `timeoutMs: 5000` in the resolveToolDomain call.

Define a constant DOMAIN_RESOLVE_TIMEOUT_MS = 5_000 near the top of mcp-tool-dispatch.ts.
